coredata.dc:title: Fluorine-doped carbon dots (F-CDs) adsorbing DNA via hydrophobic interaction play dual-role of quenching carrier and signal reference for ratiometric fluorescence strategy to detect microRNA

coredata.dc:description: The expression levels of microRNAs (miRNA) can provide insights into the occurrence, progression, and treatment efficacy in tumors, highlighting the need for precise and easily controllable detection methodologies. The reasonable design of carbon dots enables the precise manipulation of luminescence and surface characteristics. Herein, fluorine doped carbon dots (F-CDs) were designed and prepared to establish a reasonable control of the interaction force between DNA and F-CDs. F-CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal reaction involving tetrafluoroterephthalic acid and tetraethylene pentaamine, resulting in noteworthy optical and amphiphilic properties. F-CDs proficiently interact with single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) through hydrophobic interaction, leading to the quenching of dye labeled ssDNA, while maintaining self-fluorescence stability. The quenching mechanism was elucidated. The complementary DNA sequence induced nearly 100% desorption of ssDNA from F-CDs. Assisted by the target cycling signal amplification from duplex specific nuclease (DSN) and the self-fluorescence of F-CDs, an effective ratiometric strategy utilizing the adsorption system of F-CDs and probe DNA for let-7a was established for detecting let-7a, a critical miRNA in the assessment of tumor development. Under the optimized conditions, this method demonstrated high sensitivity and reproducibility in detecting let-7a with the linear range from 1.0 fM to 10.0 nM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 fM. The recovery of let-7a in plasma and the evaluation of let-7a levels in seven cell types underscored the method's potential for efficient applications in monitoring of miRNA levels in real samples.

(C) UV–vis absorption spectrum and the maximum exciting and emission spectra of F-CDs. (D) FL emission spectra of F-CDs with different exciting wavelengths. (E) FTIR spectrum and (F) XPS spectrum of the F-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum of F-CDs for C1s (G), N1s (H), O1s (I), and F1s (insert in F).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of F-CDs, pDNA, mixture of F-CDs and pDNA, and mixture of F-CDs, pDNA and BSA with exciting at 488 nm and 365 nm; (B) Quenching efficiency of F-CDs on FAM-labeled ssDNA with different lengths and base compositions. (C) The change of fluorescence intensities of 60 µg/mL F-CDs and FAM-15 T at different reaction period. Comparison of the fluorescence intensity of the quenching systems containing 60 µg/mL F-CDs and 50 nM pDNA at the beginning (D) and 1.5 months (E). (F) Fluorescence spectra of 50 nM pure FAM in the absence and presence of 60 μg/mL F-CDs.

(A) Spectral overlaps between the UV–vis spectrum of F-CDs and the fluorescence emission spectrum of pDNA. (B) Fluorescence lifetime of FAM in pDNA alone (black) and pDNA/F-CDs complex (red). (C) Spectral overlaps between fluorescence emission spectrum of F-CDs and the UV–vis spectrum of pDNA. (D) Cyclic voltammogram of F-CDs. (E) The UV–vis spectrum of F-CDs (Tauc plot in the insert). (F) the scheme of the electron transfer from the excited state of FAM to F-CDs resulting in the suppression of the fluorescence of the FAM and stable fluorescence of F-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence anisotropy of FAM in the absence and presence of cDNA with or without 50 nM pDNA incubation with F-CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra with exciting at 488 nm (B) and 365 nm (C) of pDNA, and the system containing 60 μg/mL F-CDs and 50 nM pDNA in the presence of different concentrations of cDNA. (D) The linear relationship between cDNA concentration and FFAM/FF-CDs from (B) and (C). Error bars represented the SDs of three parallel determinations.

(A) Scheme of the ratiometric fluorescence platform for let-7a based on F-CDs with DSN-assisted amplification. (B) Feasibility of fluorescence platform based on F-CDs and DSN-assisted amplification for let-7a detection. Fluorescence spectrum：(a) F-CDs+pDNA, (b) F-CDs+pDNA+DSN, (c) F-CDs+pDNA+let-7a, (d) F-CDs+pDNA+let-7a+DSN, (e) pDNA. [pDNA]= 50 nM, [let-7a]= 20 nM, [DSN]= 0.1 U, [F-CDs]= 60 μg/mL. (C) The emission spectra of FAM under the condition of different concentrations of let-7a. (D) The emission spectra of F-CDs under the condition of different concentrations of let-7a. (E) The relationship between FFAM/FF-CDs and let-7a concentration. (F) The change of FFAM/FF-CDs under the existence of target let-7a, miRNA-21, miRNA-126 and miRNA-155 respectively. Error bars represented the SDs of three parallel determinations.
Relative expression of let-7a in 7 kinds of cells quantified by the (A) ratiometric fluorescence method and (B) qRT-PCR. Error bars represented the SDs of three parallel determinations.

head.ce:title: Fluorine-doped carbon dots (F-CDs) adsorbing DNA via hydrophobic interaction play dual-role of quenching carrier and signal reference for ratiometric fluorescence strategy to detect microRNA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The expression levels of microRNAs (miRNA) can provide insights into the occurrence, progression, and treatment efficacy in tumors, highlighting the need for precise and easily controllable detection methodologies. The reasonable design of carbon dots enables the precise manipulation of luminescence and surface characteristics. Herein, fluorine doped carbon dots (F-CDs) were designed and prepared to establish a reasonable control of the interaction force between DNA and F-CDs. F-CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal reaction involving tetrafluoroterephthalic acid and tetraethylene pentaamine, resulting in noteworthy optical and amphiphilic properties. F-CDs proficiently interact with single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) through hydrophobic interaction, leading to the quenching of dye labeled ssDNA, while maintaining self-fluorescence stability. The quenching mechanism was elucidated. The complementary DNA sequence induced nearly 100% desorption of ssDNA from F-CDs. Assisted by the target cycling signal amplification from duplex specific nuclease (DSN) and the self-fluorescence of F-CDs, an effective ratiometric strategy utilizing the adsorption system of F-CDs and probe DNA for let-7a was established for detecting let-7a, a critical miRNA in the assessment of tumor development. Under the optimized conditions, this method demonstrated high sensitivity and reproducibility in detecting let-7a with the linear range from 1.0 fM to 10.0 nM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 fM. The recovery of let-7a in plasma and the evaluation of let-7a levels in seven cell types underscored the method's potential for efficient applications in monitoring of miRNA levels in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) represent a class of endogenous small noncoding RNAs (around 22 nucleotides) that directly bind to their corresponding mRNA, thereby inhibiting the expression of target genes [1,2]. Notably, miRNA levels, especially let-7a, offer insights into tumor developmental lineage, differentiation status, disease progression, and drug resistance in malignancies, including liver, nasopharyngeal, and lung cancer [3–5]. Consequently, the detection of miRNA holds significance in the diagnosis and assessment of cancer [6,7]. Consequently, the development of a straightforward, rapid, reproducible, and selective analytical platform for miRNAs assumes importance for practical clinical diagnosis. Compared with the methods such as northern blotting [8], quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) [9], and gene microarrays [10], fluorescent methods have garnered prominence in miRNA analysis owing to the characteristics of user-friendliness, sensitivity and quick response [11–13]. Additionally, the thoughtful design of fluorescent probes can introduce ratiometric strategies to fluorescence methods, thereby enhancing the stability and reproducibility of testing results [14].

Several fluorescent sensors have emerged for nucleic acid detection, leveraging DNA probe adsorption on nanomaterials [15,16] and employing enzyme-assisted target cycling approaches [17,18]. In contrast to covalent immobilization [18], the physisorption of DNA onto nanomaterials have gained widespread popularity for biomolecules detection due to its simplicity and cost-effectiveness [19,20]. Various nanomaterials, including graphene oxide (GO) [19], metal oxides [21], metal organic frameworks [22], and gold nanoparticles [23], strongly adsorb DNA probes through variable interaction forces, significantly quench the dye-labeled fluorescence. This quenching platform is harnessed to detect complementary DNA (cDNA) or aptamer targets, contingent on the distinct adsorption behaviors of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) [11–14,24]. The introduction of enzyme-assisted signal amplification is gradually applied for miRNA detection [17,18]. Recently, the approaches that selective cleaving DNA probe in DNA/miRNA heteroduplex by enzymes, like duplex specific nuclease (DSN), will induce signal amplification for enhancing performance by target cycling. Despite advancements in the integration of enzyme-assisted amplification and DNA adsorption on carriers, certain challenges persist in improving the practicality and efficacy. Achieving a 100% release of DNA remains challenging due to carriers' potent adsorption effect on DNA probes, which can constrain the formation of heteroduplex and potentially impact the enzymatic activity and the efficiency of enzyme-assisted target cycling [23]. The design and implementation of carriers capable of achieving both effective adsorption and reversible release present a promising avenue for the sensitive detection of target miRNA, combining a fluorescence platform and enzyme-assisted amplification.

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a prominent fluorescent material, celebrated for their versatile applications and controllable preparations [25]. Carbon-based materials possessing hydrophilic and hydrophobic traits have garnered increasing attention due to their affinity for biphasic interfaces [26–28]. However, methods for creating amphiphilic CDs remain relatively limited in scope [29]. Amphiphilic carbon nanomaterials containing carbon dots are typically generated using modifications involving hydrophobic groups on hydrophilic materials [30], reactions involving both hydrophilic and hydrophobic reactants [31], surfactant-mediated reactions [32], or the incorporation of amphiphilic ligands [33]. The resulting amphiphilic CDs are primarily applied for fluorescence-based determination of ions and bacteria due to their optical properties [34,35]. There is a scarcity of research focusing on the combined utilization of both the optical properties and the surface characteristics of amphiphilic CDs. Hydrophobic interactions stand as a commonly employed and effective means for nanomaterials to interact with biomolecules [36–38]. Heteroelement doping is an effective way to synthesize CDs. CDs doped with fluorine (F) through bottom-up synthesis significantly impact the surface, structural and optical features of CDs for enlarging usefulness [39,40]. For instance, F doping can induce numerous surface defects on CDs for achieving piezochromic behavior [40]. The doping of F in CDs improves of the Stokes shift of CDs for the in vivo drug monitoring with the heightened anti-interference ability [41]. Although heteroelement-doped CDs exhibit tuned surface properties for adsorbing potential biomacromolecule [38,42], the utilizing of heteroelement doping with the dual applications of the luminescent performance and interacting platform to biomacromolecules is still lack in preparing functional CDs.

In this work, amphiphilic fluorine-doped carbon dots (F-CDs) with green emission were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using tetrafluoroterephthalic acid and tetraethylene pentamine. F-CDs exhibited the hydrophilic characteristics and the hydrophobicity ascribed to the C-F bond. Hydrophobic interaction from the hydrophobicity of F-CDs provided the main driving force for adsorbing ssDNA on F-CDs. Meanwhile, F-CDs efficiently, universally and rapidly quenched dye-labeled ssDNA and maintained the stability of autofluorescence for use as a signal reference in a ratiometric approach. The quenching system, constructed by adsorption of DNA on F-CDs, demonstrated robust storage stability and completely reversible desorption. Considering the significance of quantitatively detecting let-7a in the diagnosis of associated cancers and in the study of its role in cancer cell function [3–5], the ratiometric fluorescence detection of let-7a was achieved based on the adsorption system of F-CDs and FAM labeled probe with sensitivity and practicality. This work provides an insight of exploring the hydrophobic interaction between CDs and DNA sequences to fully exploit the establishment of an effective fluorescence strategy based on CDs’ luminescence and interaction platforms.

All DNA and miRNA sequences containing FAM-labeled different ssDNA (Table S1) were obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. The nucleotide sequences mentioned were listed in Table S1. Tetrafluoroterephthalic acid, tetraethylenepentamine, sodium pyrophosphate, and adenosine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. NaOH, NaCl, urea, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tween 80, and triton X-100 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. DSN was purchased from Evrogen (Moscow, Russia). Total-RNA extraction kits were purchased from Beijing Zoman Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). The DEPC-treated water, RNase-free tips and tubes were used throughout the work. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (Milli-Q, 18 MΩ·cm).

The fluorescence spectra were carried out on Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on UV2450 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation) in the range of 200 nm to 800 nm. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Talos F200S, Thermo Fisher, USA) was work at accelerating voltage of 200 kV for the morphology evaluation of F-CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA) was carried out in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 by KBr tableting technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher, USA) was performed to characterize the structure of F-CDs using an Al Kα X-ray beam. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) with CuKα radiation was carried out in the range of 5–90°.

Fifteen milliliters of 50 mM tetrafluoroterephthalic acid was mixed with 5 mL of tetraethylenepentaamine. After stirring for 15 min, the mixture was heated at 180 ℃ for 24 h. The reacted mixture was left at room temperature for one week. Using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, the product was dialyzed in water for two days. Afterward, the product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 ℃ for subsequent use.

The distinct adsorption capacities onto F-CDs for ssDNA and dsDNA were investigated using fluorescence anisotropy (FA) detection. The fluorescence intensity at 365 nm and 500 nm for each sample was measured in four directions (HH, HV, VH, VV) by FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, Britain). The FA value (r) was calculated according to the following equations [30,31].
G =
IHV
IHH
r =
IVV − GIVH
IVV + 2 GIVH

I was the fluorescence intensity; the first angle mark (V or H) indicated the position of the polarizer in the direction of the excited light. The second angle mark (V or H) represented the position of the polarizer in the direction of the emitted light. The V and H represented the vertical and horizontal directions of the polarizer, respectively. The I
VV suggested the intensity of vertically polarized emitted light detected under the excitation of vertically polarized light. G was the ratio between I HV and I HH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and desorption of DNA on F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of DNA was studied by adding 50 µg/mL of F-CDs and FAM labeled-ssDNA with different bases and lengths (including pDNA, 15 A, 15 T, 15 C, 15 G, 20 C, 20 T, R1 and R2 in Table S1). The emission behavior of FAM was monitored. Mediated by denaturants and surfactants, the ssDNA desorption from F-CDs was investigated to elucidate the anchoring force of ssDNA on F-CDs [43]. Sixty µg/mL F-CDs and 10 µg/mL graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets were mixed with 100 nM pDNA in 200 µL PBS for 10 min, respectively. In this setup, GO served as a classic reference carrier for quenching [23]. The above adsorption solution was then divided into two parts: one was supplemented with PBS to reach a final volume of 200 µL and served as the blank, and the other part was independently dispersed in 100 µL of 3 M NaCl, 10 mM NaOH, 10 mM pyrophosphate, 1 mM adenosine, 5 M urea and 50% DMSO. Subsequent fluorescence intensity scanning was conducted continuously over 1 h. According to the similar above procedure, the reaction system was supplemented with 0.01% SDS, 0.01% CTAB, 0.01% Triton X-100%, and 0.01% Tween 80 individually to assess their effects on the desorption interaction between F-CDs and pDNA. In addition, a comparative evaluation of complementary DNA (cDNA)-mediated desorption was carried out by introducing varying concentrations of cDNA into the quenching system, which involved probe DNA for let-7a (pDNA) on F-CDs or GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing let-7a based on the adsorption system of pDNA and F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 10 µL of pDNA (50 nM) and 20 µL of F-CDs (60 µg/mL) was mixed and interacted for 10 min (F-CDs/pDNA). Then, 1 µL of RNase inhibitor (40 U/µL), 5 µL of DSN buffer (10 ×), 4 µL of RNase-free water, and 10 µL of let-7a with different concentrations, were introduced into the above mixture. This mixture was incubated at 60 ℃ for 30 min. Then, 3 µL of DSN (0.05 U/µL) was added for continuous reaction at 60 ℃ for 1 h. It was terminated using DSN terminating solution (2 ×) at 60 ℃ for 5 min. Following this, 145 µL of PBS was added into the above solution for measuring fluorescence spectra of both F-CDs and FAM with exciting at 365 nm and 488 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing let-7a in plasma and different cell samples
ce:section.ce:para: Approved by the Ethics Committee of the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University (Fuzhou, China), human blood in EDTA anticoagulation tube obtained from healthy volunteer with informed consent was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was diluted by Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 8.0) to be 1%. The diluted plasma was applied to test the spiked let-7a with different concentrations based on the procedure illustrated in Section 2.5.

Seven kinds of cells, like the normal nasopharyngeal cells (NPG-1), nasopharyngeal carcinoma cell lines (SUNE, CNE, HONE, C666–1), human lung adenocarcinoma cells (H1975), and human hepatoma cells (HepG2) were collected for total RNA extraction using the RNA extraction kit. 100 ng high-purity total RNA extraction was added to the adsorption system of ssDNA and F-CDs. Following the Section 2.5, the let-7a expressions in the above cells were detected through the ratiometric fluorescence sensor and compared with qRT-PCR.

The TEM image of F-CDs ( Fig. 1 A) and the size distribution revealed that F-CDs exhibited a uniform spherical morphology, with an average diameter of approximately 2.25 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) illustrated that the lattice fringe spacing of prepared F-CDs was about 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, suggesting that F-CDs possess a graphite-like structure [44]. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of F-CDs (Fig. 1B) showed two broad diffraction peaks at 28.8° and 36–48°, indicating the arrangement of carbon lattice in a disordered fashion due to the amorphous nature [45]. The optical properties of F-CDs were investigated. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum displayed a wide absorption band at 365 nm and a shoulder peak at 290 nm, attributed to the n-π * transition of CO and the π-π * transition of the sp2 domain (CC) (Fig. 1 C) [40,41]. Exciting at 365 nm, F-CDs emitted fluorescence (Fig. 1 C) centered at 500 nm, displaying a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 5.46% relative to quinoline sulfate (Fig. S1A). The emission peaks of F-CDs exhibited a gradual red shift with increasing excitation wavelengths from 330 to 430 nm (Fig. 1D), suggesting the excitation-dependent property. The biocompatibility of F-CDs was evaluated by using an in vivo cell model based on the standard CCK-8 kit. As shown in Fig. S1B, the cell survival rates of L-02 (normal human liver cells) exceeded 85.7% when co-cultured with different concentrations of F-CDs (25–400 μg/mL) for 24 h, respectively, suggesting the little toxicity of F-CDs to cells.

The photostability of F-CDs to various environmental conditions was monitored. F-CDs retained over 93% of the initial intensity with ranging temperatures from 5 to 95 ℃ (Fig. S2A). In addition, F-CDs exhibited stable fluorescence performance in high ionic environments (1 M) (Fig. S2B) and across a broad pH range from 3 to 10 (Fig. S2C). Besides the exceptional long-term storage stability within 5 months (Fig. S2D), the fluorescence intensities of F-CDs were basically constant during the continuous exposure to 300 W xenon lamp for 90 min (Fig. S3). In contrast, two other commercial organic dyes (FAM and ROX) experienced significant bleaching under the same conditions (Fig. S3). These findings clarify that F-CDs possess stable fluorescence properties in diverse environments.

The surface composition and chemical characteristics of F-CDs were assessed utilizing FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1E, the FTIR peaks at 2841 and 2946 cm−1 were attributed to the C-H stretching vibration of methyl/methylene. Furthermore, peaks linked to the C-N stretching vibration at approximately 1403 cm−1, N-H or O-H stretching vibrations at 3422 cm−1, and C-O at 1129 cm−1 were identified. Moreover, bands at 1661 and 1738 cm−1 represented the CC stretching vibrations and the CO stretching vibrations of aromatic carboxylic acids. In the XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 F), binding energies at 284.82, 398.74, 530.90, and 685.57 eV were corresponded to C1s, N1s, O1s and F1s, respectively, with atomic percentages of 60.3%, 31.63%, 7.17% and 0.9%. Specifically, high-resolution XPS analysis of C1s (Fig. 1 G) can be deconvoluted into four different peaks, which were attributed to C-C/CC (284.4 eV), C-N (285.4 eV), C-O (286.1 eV) and CO/C-F (287.4 eV), respectively [46,47]. The N1s spectra (Fig. 1H) demonstrated three binding energies at 398.6, 399.3 and 400.2 eV, which were assigned as pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and N-H, respectively [48]. The two peaks of the O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1I) can be presumably fitted with CO (530.8 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (531.9 eV) [49]. These results illustrate the doping effect of F and the hydrophilic bonds of -NH2, -OH, and OC-OH on the surface of F-CDs. Zeta potential testing of F-CDs in water indicated a surface charge of − 4.89 mV (data not shown), indicating a negatively charged surface. Furthermore, the high-resolution F1s XPS spectrum (insert in Fig. 1 F) showed a single peak centered at 685.6 eV, corresponding to semi-ionic C-F bond, which is a typical hydrophobic functional group [50]. Combined with hydrophilic bonds on the surface of F-CDs, the presence of C-F confers the certain degree of hydrophobicity in amphiphilic F-CDs [50]. The contact angle (CA) testing was carried out on a concentrated F-CDs thin film deposited on mica (Fig. S4), yielding an approximate CA of 52°. This collectively highlights the coexistence of hydrophobic and hydrophilic characteristics in F-CDs [26].

The potential luminous variabilities of F-CDs were investigated in several solvents with differing polarities. Fig. S5A and S5B demonstrated that F-CDs could be homogeneously dispersed in polar and moderately polar solvents of water, DMSO, methanol, acetone, and ethanol. However, F-CDs exhibited limited dispersal in nonpolar and weakly polar solvents, including chloroform, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, toluene, cyclohexane, and pentahexane (Fig. S5A, S5B). Regarding spectral characteristics, the emission peaks of F-CDs experienced a blue-shift of 32 nm in dimethyl sulfoxide and acetone compared to that observed in water, while the blue-shift range was 19 nm in methanol and ethanol (Fig. S5C). This shift indicates a solvatochromic effect, signifying the sensitivity of F-CDs' emission to the surrounding solvent. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of F-CDs in solvents with lower polarity were notably higher than that in aqueous solutions, following the trend of DMSO > acetone > methanol > ethanol > water (Fig. S5C). The fluorescence enhancement mechanism was analyzed through time-dependent single electron counting (TCSPC). The fluorescence lifetime of F-CDs in non-aqueous solvents showed a slight increase compared to that in water (Fig. S5D). The reduced fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime of F-CDs in water are attributed to the stronger dielectric shielding effect imposed by the hydrophobicity of F-CDs, which hindered charge transfer in an aqueous environment [51]. The dispersion and fluorescence behavior of F-CDs across solvents with varying polarities further confirm their amphiphilic nature.

The evaluation of DNA adsorption by F-CDs was conducted. The FAM-labeled ssDNA with varied lengths and bases (Table S1) were applied for the investigation using the quenching degree of the labeled dye. As shown in Fig. 2 A, pDNA (the representative of FAM-labeled ssDNA) exhibited the emission peak centered at 520 nm under 488 nm excitation. While, the emission spectrum was significantly quenched by adding F-CDs. Meanwhile, the distinct emission of F-CDs, centered at 500 nm when excited at 365 nm, remained unaffected both in the absence and presence of pDNA. Furthermore, F-CDs demonstrated effective adsorption and quenching capabilities for ssDNA with different base compositions and lengths (Fig. 2B). F-CDs adsorbed and quenched FAM labeled ssDNA with 15 bases with quenching efficiency (QE) ranging from 71.6% to 92.5% in the sequence of 15 T > 15 A≈ 15 C > 15 G (Fig. 2B). This substantiates the universal and remarkable adsorption ability of F-CDs to various ssDNA sequences. Additionally, the phenomenon of greater QE for longer DNA sequences (Fig. 2B) underscored the length-dependent adsorption capacity of F-CDs. The kinetics of adsorption (Fig. 2 C) revealed that QE achieved 89% in 10 s and 90.2% at 10 min after adding F-CDs. This pattern suggests the great potential of F-CDs as a quenching carrier [23]. Encouragingly, F-CDs could quench other fluorescent dyes labeled ssDNA (Fig. S6). The QEs of Cy5, TAMRA, and ROX were 65.50%, 71.65% and 94.05% (Fig. S6), respectively. This variation in QEs among fluorescent probes could likely be attributed to their distinct luminescence properties arising from the different energy levels.

The adsorption stability of F-CDs to DNA was assessed. The storage of the quenching system of F-CDs/pDNA at 4 ℃ for 45 days remained the QE consistently in the fluctuation between 91.1% and 92.6% (Figs. 2D, 2E). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), an effective desorption protein for non-specific DNA, was applied as the model to evaluate the possible compete adsorption of proteins onto the adsorbed system of F-CDs and ssDNA. Fig. 2 A illustrated that the fluorescence intensities remained virtually unchanged upon the addition of 100 μg/mL BSA in the quenching system, suggesting the minimal desorption of ssDNA induced by BSA. Therefore, the sustained adsorption stability over time and the resistance against protein-induced DNA dissociation of the adsorption system of F-CDs and ssDNA could hold promise for their application in the detection of biological samples.

Generally, the adsorption of dye-labeled ssDNA on F-CDs resulted in quenching of fluorescent probe with unconspicuous change in F-CDs’ self-fluorescence. The quenching mechanism of dye caused by F-CDs was discussed. Firstly, as shown in Fig. 2 F, the emission intensity and spectrum of FAM remained unchangeable as F-CDs mixed with only FAM, proving the crucial role of ssDNA in this quenching process. The quenching mechanism arises from F-CDs effectively adsorbing dye-labeled ssDNA through interactions, subsequently leading to substantial quenching via energy transfer and/or electron transfer processes, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [52] and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [53]. For validating the quenching mechanism of F-CDs to dyes, like the applied FAM, the possibility of FRET was firstly investigated. In Fig. 3 A, partial spectral overlap occurred between the emission spectrum of pDNA and the absorption spectrum of F-CDs. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra displayed that the fluorescence lifetime of FAM decreased from 4.14 ns to 3.79 ns with introducing F-CDs, indicating the existence of the dynamic quenching effect (Fig. 3B). This suggests that FRET could be one of the contributors to FAM quenching. Importantly, the emission spectrum of F-CDs overlapped with the absorption spectrum of FAM-labeled ssDNA (Fig. 3 C), yet the fluorescence behavior of F-CDs remained stable. This indicates the absence of FRET from F-CDs' emission to FAM's excitation. The electron transfer mechanism was evaluated using the HOMO-LUMO orbital theory. The EHOMO and ELUMO of F-CDs were obtained according to the empirical formula by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 3D) [54]. The measured ERed of F-CDs was − 0.46 eV (Fig. 3D), and then ELUMO was calculated to be − 3.94 eV. The band gap (Eg) of F-CDs was calculated to be 5.44 eV via UV–vis spectrum and Tauc plot (Fig. 3E), and EHOMO of F-CDs was thereby − 9.39 eV. The ELUMO of FAM from the reported work illustrates that it is higher than that of F-CDs [55]. The electron-hole theory illustrates that the anthracene oxide of FAM is prone to transfer electron when excited at 488 nm [53]. F is a strong electronegativity element. The doping effect of F in F-CDs could endow the strong ability of adsorbing adjacent electrons [56]. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 3 F, we held the mechanism that electrons of excited FAM can transfer to electron-deficient and lower ELUMO F-CDs during the interaction of F-CDs with FAM, and hence cause the quenching of FAM. Correspondingly, F-CDs in the excited state cannot transfer electrons to the ELUMO of FAM in the ground state due to the high-level ELUMO of FAM. The fluorescence of F-CDs remained stable since no FRET occurred from F-CDs to FAM with the coexistence of F-CDs and FAM (Fig. 3 F). Therefore, it was feasible to transfer electrons from the ground state of FAM to F-CDs. Briefly, adsorbed FAM can be in the excited state after absorbing the excitation wavelength of 488 nm, and then the electrons can be transferred to F-CDs with high electron receiving. As a result, the fluorescence of FAM is quenched with unchangeable fluorescence of F-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modes of interaction of F-CDs with ssDNA
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption mechanisms of ssDNA onto F-CDs were meticulously investigated by drawing comparisons with the classical adsorbing system of GO and ssDNA. DNA detachment from carriers can also occur under condition that weaken their interactions when disruptor such as DMSO was added [43]. The fluorescence responses were monitored after these disruptors were added into the quenching solution. The addition of PBS or 3 M NaCl caused no recover of fluorescence of the quenching system, conforming the weak electrostatic interaction existed in F-CDs/FAM-ssDNA system (Fig. S7B, S7B). When 10 mM NaOH was introduced, the desorption rate of FAM-ssDNA from F-CDs was twice that of GO (Fig. S7C). It may be due to that the increase in the negative charge density of F-CDs under high pH conditions, which enhanced the electrostatic repulsion leading to desorption. Additionally, the alkaline environment led to deprotonation of hydrogen bond donors in F-CDs, disrupting hydrogen bonds and further facilitating DNA desorption [57]. Moreover, investigations into potential interactions were carried out by introducing 10 mM phosphate and 1 mM adenosine (Fig. S7D, S7E). The presence of phosphate resulted in approximately 70% desorption of FAM-ssDNA from F-CDs, whereas no significant fluorescence recovery occurred upon the addition of adenosine. This observation suggests an interaction between the phosphate skeleton of DNA and F-CDs. The addition of 5 M urea led to about 40% desorption of FAM-ssDNA from F-CDs, which was weaker than the desorption from GO (Fig. S7F), indicating the relatively low degree of hydrogen bonding. Compared to GO/ssDNA with a desorption rate of 36% (Fig. S8), fluorescence was significantly enhanced in the F-CDs/ssDNA system with a desorption rate of 72% ascribed to the added 50% DMSO, highlighting a prominent hydrophobic interaction in the adsorption of ssDNA on F-CDs.

The influence of common surfactants (SDS, CTAB, Tween 80 and Triton X-100) on the F-CDs/ssDNA system were also investigated (Fig. S9). As a small molecular anionic surfactant, 0.01% SDS made ssDNA completely free from F-CDs with significantly difference from that of GO, while cationic surfactant with the same concentration (CTAB) replaced 30% of ssDNA from F-CDs and was also higher than GO (Fig. S9A, S9B). The results suggested the presence of obvious van der Waals forces between F-CDs and ssDNA [43]. Interesting to note, the strong desorption of ssDNA from F-CDs when using ionic surfactant indicated that such surfactants not only reduce the van der Waals forces, but also weaken F-CDs' adsorption of ssDNA through their hydrophobic groups [58]. On the contrary, two non-ionic macromolecular surfactants, 0.01% Triton X- 100% and 0.01% Tween 80, caused weak desorption of ssDNA, while the desorption rate for GO was about 15% and 35%, respectively (Fig. S9C, S9D). This phenomenon may be due to the weakness of Van der Waals force between surfactant and F-CDs, which is diametrically opposite to GO adsorbing system. In summary, the strength of the interaction between F-CDs and ssDNA specifically includes the following order: van der Waals forces>hydrophobic interactions>phosphate skeleton interactions>hydrogen bonding interactions. Due to van der Waals forces being a prevalent mode of action each other, hydrophobic interaction plays a significant role in the adsorption of ssDNA on F-CDs. This interaction confirms the amphiphilic property of F-CDs and underscores the importance of hydrophobicity in their adsorption of ssDNA, especially for the negatively charged F-CDs. Importantly, the occurrence of hydrophobic interaction between nanomaterials and ssDNA is rare [59], highlighting the uniqueness of the F-CDs' behavior. This also suggests that various CDs can be obtained through specific preparation methods, broadening the possibilities for establishing biological applications based on CDs with diverse properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The difference in the effect of F-CDs on ssDNA and dsDNA and its feasibility for miRNA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence anisotropy (FA) serves as a powerful indicator of molecular interactions between DNA and nanomaterials [42]. Typically, when a fluorescent probe adheres to a nanomaterial, it exhibits a high FA; conversely, FA decreases when the probe is distanced from the nanomaterial. Consequently, FA measurements were conducted to assess the binding capability of F-CDs to both ssDNA and dsDNA. As depicited in Fig. 4 A, the free pDNA exhibited a minor and consistent FA value (0.03) both in the presence and absence of cDNA (Table S1) without F-CDs. While, FAM-ssDNA displayed a significantly higher FA value (0.14) in the presence of F-CDs compared to that of FAM-ssDNA and cDNA with F-CDs, as well as the unbound FAM-ssDNA (with an FA of 0.06). The notable decrease in FA of pDNA and cDNA with F-CDs indicates the release of pDNA due to the formation of dsDNA, suggesting distinct interactions of F-CDs with ssDNA and dsDNA. This phenomenon implies a basis for target gene detection through hybridization using F-CDs as carriers. However, the ability of the probe to desorb from the quenching carrier after forming a double strand with complementary sequences is also a crucial factor affecting DNA detection performance [23]. Correspondingly, as a classic quenching agent to dye labeled ssDNA, GO was employed to compare the release rate of probe DNA by complementary sequences [23]. As shown in Fig. 4B, with the addition of different concentrations of cDNA, increased cDNA caused the fluorescence of labeled FAM gradually recover. While, the fluorescence intensities of F-CDs at 500 nm were kept with increased cDNA (Fig. 4 C). When the concentration of cDNA reached 6 µM, the fluorescence intensity reached the initial degree of pDNA, suggesting the 100% desorption ability by the formed dsDNA. This indicates that the adsorption and desorption of DNA by F-CDs is reversible. A ratiometric fluorescent strategy for target gene detection can be established by measuring the variable fluorescence of FAM and the stable fluorescence of F-CDs through ratiometric mode (FFAM/FF-CDs) with a linear equation of y = 0.4799 + 2.6119 CcDNA (R2 =0.9914) (Fig. 4D). Notably, the desorption time for both F-CDs and GO was approximately 15 min. However, cDNA could only release around 70% of ssDNA due to GO's strong adsorption ability (Fig. S10). The enhanced and reversible desorption capability of F-CDs will enhance testing efficacy when utilized as carriers. Furthermore, the intrinsic fluorescence of F-CDs can serve as an internal reference for ratiometric approaches.

Leveraging the distinctive characteristics of F-CDs, we formulated a hypothesis and proceeded to evaluate the feasibility of a ratiometric strategy employing FAM-ssDNA and F-CDs for let-7a detection facilitated by DSN-assisted target recycling ( Fig. 5 A). The fluorescence intensities of FAM at 521 nm were measured at various stages to evaluate this approach. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity was lowest when mixing F-CDs and pDNA, indicating the adsorption and quenching of pDNA by F-CDs (column A). Upon the addition of DSN (column B), a modest increase in signal affirmed the slight activity of DSN on the adsorbed ssDNA by F-CDs. Furthermore, the F-CDs/pDNA/let-7a system (column C) exhibited a heightened fluorescence response, attributed to the formation of double-stranded structures involving pDNA and let-7a. However, the hybridization efficiency of low concentrations of let-7a was limited, resulting in a relatively modest fluorescence response. Subsequently, a significant enhancement in fluorescence response was observed in the F-CDs/pDNA mixture containing let-7a and DSN (column D), where the signal was 2.9 times stronger than that of the system without DSN. This outcome highlighted the successful selective digestion of pDNA within the pDNA/let-7a hybrid by DSN. The DSN digestion released let-7a from the duplex, initiating the next round of interactions and amplification, thereby leading to signal amplification. Consequently, the integration of DSN-assisted target amplification with the quenching effect by F-CDs proved to be a viable platform for miRNA sensing.

As an enzyme-mediated reaction, some experimental factors for enzyme performance were optimized (Fig. S11). The fluorescence signal plateaued when the enzyme was 0.15 U (Fig. S11A), demonstrating that 0.15 U of DSN can ensure a sufficient change. The effect of digestion time and incubation temperature was also examined. Prolonging the digestion time from 20 to 120 min led to a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity, with the signal stabilizing after 60 min (Fig. S11B). Similarly, the highest fluorescence signal was observed at 60 ℃ (Fig. S11C). Consequently, conditions involving 0.15 U of DSN, a temperature of 60 ℃, and a digestion time of 60 min were optimal for DSN-assisted target recycling.

Fluorescence responses were measured for both FAM and F-CDs across various concentrations of let-7a under the optimized conditions. FAM's response gradually increased with increased let-7a (Fig. 5 C), while F-CDs' response remained relatively constant (Fig. 5D). Accordingly, a robust linear relationship was established between the ratios (FFAM/FF-CDs) and the concentration of let-7a within the range of 10.0 fM to 10.0 nM (Fig. 5E). The fitting equation was Y= 0.59957 + 0.11134 ×Clet-7a with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9949. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.0 fM using the 3σ/S (σwas the standard deviation of the blank (n = 9) and S was the slope of the linear equation). A summarized comparison of the analytical performance of fluorescent methods based on DSN-mediated and nanomaterial quenching designs was illustrated in Table S2. The comparison suggests the method's exceptional detection sensitivity and its straightforward operational process. The specificity and reproducibility of this method were assessed. Compared with the three different interfering miRNAs, let-7a produced a pronounced fluorescence response than the weak responses of the interfering miRNAs (Fig. 5 F). In addition, the reproducibility of this method was evaluated by detecting 0.01 nM and 0.1 nM of let-7a through six consecutive measurements. The RSDs of the measurements were 2.0% and 2.9% for 0.01 nM and 0.1 nM of let-7a, respectively. These results underscore the excellent specificity and reproducibility of this method. To explore the applicability of the developed ratiometric strategy for let-7a detection, the recovery experiments were performed in human plasma. As shown in Table S3, satisfactory recoveries ranging from 94.8% to 100.9% with the RSDs less than 3.5% were obtained in spiked let-7a, suggesting the potential of this method in detecting actual biological samples.

Expanding the utility of the ratiometric fluorescence biosensor, we proceeded to assess the relative expression levels of let-7a in seven distinct cell types, encompassing both normal and cancerous cells. These cell types included NPG-1, SUNE, CNE, HONE, C666–1, H1975, and HepG2. Additionally, the let-7a expressions in the aforementioned cell groups were quantitatively measured and juxtaposed via qRT-PCR ( Fig. 6). The distribution of let-7a expression levels determined using the ratiometric method closely mirrored the patterns obtained from qPCR, demonstrating a concordance between the two methodologies. These outcomes underscore the potential of the ratiometric method founded on F-CDs for effectively distinguishing cancerous cells from their normal counterparts based on the discernible variations in the signal ratios (FFAM/FF-CDs) (Fig. 6).

In summary, the synthesis of amphiphilic negatively charged F-CDs with green fluorescence was achieved through a straightforward hydrothermal method. F-CDs can strongly adsorb ssDNA through Van der Waals force and hydrophobic interaction and efficiently quench fluorescence of various dye-labeled ssDNA while keeping the inherent fluorescence stable. Remarkably, this represents the first instance of amphiphilic CDs adsorbing ssDNA mainly via hydrophobic interactions. The adsorption system exhibited robust storage stability and remarkable resistance to protein interference. By employing a ratiometric fluorescence approach, we achieved efficient let-7a detection, capitalizing on the distinct adsorption capacity of F-CDs towards ssDNA and dsDNA. Furthermore, the real detection results of let-7a expression levels showcased the promising potential of this sensing platform, where F-CDs adsorbed ssDNA, for rapid and accurate miRNA detection in clinical diagnostics.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhenzhen Wang received her MS degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis from Fujian Medical University in 2020. Her research interest is fluorescence analysis.
Yiping Chen works as a professor of treating liver cancer and the drug resistance monitor in the department of Interventional Radiology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University.

Menghan Zhang received her BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2022. She is currently a MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research focuses on pharmaceutical analysis, preparation and therapy of Nanomedicines.

Linlin Xu received her BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2011. She is currently a MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research focuses on pharmaceutical analysis based on carbon dots.
Yichun Xu her MS degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis from Fujian Medical University in 2021. Her research interest is fluorescence analysis.
Li Chen her MS degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis from Fujian Medical University in 2022. Her research interest is fluorescence analysis.
Xinyang Yin his MS degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis from Fujian Medical University in 2023. His research interest is fluorescence analysis.

Shuangying Yang received her BS degree in Pharmacy from Xiamen Medical University in 2023. She is currently a MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research focuses on the development and application of carbon dots.

Shaohuang Weng earned his Ph.D. degree at the Department of Chemistry, Xiamen University, in 2009. He is a professor in the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University. From 2016–2017, he did his research about the optical sensor in Xiamen University as a visiting scholar. His research focuses on preparation and application of nanomaterials in bioanalysis.

Yanjie Zheng received her PhD at Fujian Medical University in 2016. Currently she is an associate professor in Fujian Medical University. Her research focuses on the monitoring of target microRNA for the evaluation of clinical medication.

Jianyong Huang received his MS degree at Fujian Medical University in 2014. Currently he is an associate professor in Fujian Medical University Union Hospital (China). His research focuses on the monitoring of target genes for the evaluation of clinical medication.
